Home / Leagues / NHL / 2015-16

2015-16 NHL Season

1,230 regular-season games · 16 playoff teams · 10,000 simulations

Final
Overview Regular Season Model Insights Playoffs Season Trends Game Logs

Champion

Pittsburgh Penguins

Won Stanley Cup 4-2 over San Jose Sharks

Runner-Up

San Jose Sharks

Lost Stanley Cup 4-2

Biggest Overachiever

Washington Capitals

+15.6 points above simulation

Biggest Disappointment

Montreal Canadiens

-14.6 points below simulation

Season Summary

Every team's regular-season finish compared against 10,000 simulations. Click any column header to sort; use the dropdown to filter by Conference or Division. Luck is the team's actual outcome minus the sim's mean — positive means the team beat the model. Percentile is where the actual result fell in the team's sim distribution (e.g. 90% = the team did this well or better in only 10% of sims).

Head-to-Head

Each cell shows a team's points earned in that matchup (row vs column) and below it the expected points the model predicted based on each individual game's win, OT/SO, and loss probabilities. Navy-tinted cells mean the team beat the model's expectations by more than one point in that matchup; gold-tinted cells mean they fell short by the same margin. The first matrix for each division covers within-division games; the second (when shown) covers games against teams outside that division — aggregated by division when there are many opponents, broken out team-by-team when there are few.

Scoring Distribution

Histograms of goals scored and allowed, a home-vs-away score scatter (marker size grows with how often that exact score happened), and summary stats — all driven by the team filter below. Default view is league-wide.

Points vs. Goal Differential

How goals translated into points. Teams above the cluster overperformed their goals differential (often called Pythagorean luck), while teams below underperformed. Hover for exact values.

Top Overachievers & Disappointments

The teams furthest from where the simulation said they'd land — a fast read of the Luck column on top of the main season summary above.

# Team Actual Sim Luck
1 Washington Capitals 120 104.4 +15.6
2 Pittsburgh Penguins 104 91.5 +12.5
3 Florida Panthers 103 91.8 +11.2
4 Dallas Stars 109 98.2 +10.8
5 St. Louis Blues 107 97.1 +9.9

Biggest Disappointments

# Team Actual Sim Luck

2 Vancouver Canucks 75 88.9 -13.9
3 Winnipeg Jets 78 89.6 -11.6
4 Calgary Flames 77 87.8 -10.8
5 Minnesota Wild 87 97.8 -10.8

Most Unlikely Streaks

For each team, we identify their longest winning streak and longest losing streak of the season, then compute the model's a-priori probability of that exact streak — the product of per-game win (or loss) probabilities over those specific games. The five rarest streaks of each kind appear below. A 6-game winning streak by a bad team is often more remarkable than a 10-gamer by a great one.

Most Unlikely Winning Streaks

# Team Length Dates Probability
1 Florida Panthers 12 Dec 15–Jan 10 1 in 85,745
2 Chicago Blackhawks 12 Dec 29–Jan 19 1 in 10,532
3 Anaheim Ducks 11 Feb 13–Mar 5 1 in 8,963
4 Calgary Flames 7 Dec 1–17 1 in 1,887
5 Montreal Canadiens 9 Oct 7–24 1 in 1,803

Most Unlikely Losing Streaks

# Team Length Dates Probability
1 Minnesota Wild 8 Jan 23–Feb 13 1 in 2,777
2 Vancouver Canucks 9 Mar 14–29 1 in 2,027
3 Columbus Blue Jackets 8 Oct 9–22 1 in 1,391
4 Philadelphia Flyers 6 Oct 27–Nov 5 1 in 669
5 Calgary Flames 7 Feb 21–Mar 3 1 in 457

Home-Field Advantage Edge

The simulation applies the same home-field boost to every game across the league, so the per-game home win probabilities bake in the model's idea of HFA. For each team, we add up the expected points (using win, OT, and OTL probabilities) over home games and compare to actual home points — and do the same on the road. The bar height is (home points above expected) minus (road points above expected). A tall positive bar means the team's home/road split exceeded what the model predicted — a real arena-edge effect. A tall negative bar means the reverse: they earned fewer points at home or more points on the road than expected.

Finish Position Heatmaps

Each cell is the probability — across 10,000 simulations — that the team (row) finished at that position (column). Use the buttons to switch between divisions, leagues, and the overall MLB view. Rows are sorted by the simulation's average finish (best at top).

Point Totals in Context

How did each team's actual results compare to their simulated results, Elo rating, average opponent Elo, and percentile within simulated outcomes? Use the buttons to switch between views. In each chart, dashed crosshairs at the league means split the plot into four quadrants: pastel green (team did well, model agreed), pastel red (team did poorly, model agreed), and pastel yellow (model and reality disagreed). Click a division in the legend to toggle it.

Stochastic Playoff Outcomes

Across 1,000 simulations starting from preseason Elo, every team had a chance to make the playoffs based on how the regular-season Monte Carlo played out. The default view sums those simulations up: what fraction of them had each team making the playoffs, advancing each round, and winning the title. The round buttons switch to heatmaps showing how often every pair of teams faced off in each round across all sims.

Team Made Playoffs Advanced to Divisional Round Advanced to Conference Finals Advanced to Stanley Cup Won Stanley Cup

Playoff Seed Probabilities

Across the 1,000 regular-season simulations, the probability that each team finished as each playoff seed (or missed the playoffs entirely). Rows sorted by likelihood of earning the top seed; teams that more often missed the playoffs sit toward the bottom.

Points Required to Achieve Each Seed

Empirical cumulative distribution: for each seed in each division, the probability that a team holding that seed finished the regular season with at most a given number of points. The point where each line passes through 50% is roughly the median points for that seed.

Actual Bracket Simulation

With the actual playoff bracket fixed in place, this second simulation re-runs each matchup 1,000 times and tallies how often each team advanced past each round and won the title. The right-most column shows what actually happened. Round buttons switch to matchup heatmaps for that round.

Team Advanced to Divisional Round Advanced to Conference Finals Advanced to Stanley Cup Won Stanley Cup Actual Outcome

Most-Likely Championship Paths

For each playoff team, the opponent they most often faced and defeated at each round across all bracket simulations. Each step shows two numbers: Matchup % is the fraction of this team's wins in this round that came against this opponent; Win % is the head-to-head probability they beat this opponent when the two meet. Note: fixed early-round matchups against bracket-determined opponents will show 100% Matchup — that's because those pairings are decided by the bracket.

Season Trends

How each metric evolved across the season — Elo rating, actual points, simulated final points, playoff probability, and division-title probability — day by day. Pick a division to see all five charts for its teams.
